unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit the focus is on people and work. You will explore the many different jobs and professions that keep communities running, from physicians and lawyers to food vendors and commentators. You will learn how people choose their careers, what skills different jobs require, and how the world of work connects to ideas of fairness, justice, and community responsibility.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners exploring careers, justice and community responsibility

vocabularyGroups.title: Conflict, Resolution, and Other Important Words

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The ability to make good choices and decisions based on knowledge and experience.

The word wisdom belongs to our knowledge and language vocabulary.
I can explain wisdom in my own words.
I can connect wisdom to this unit's texts.
I can use wisdom in a clear Grade 6 sentence.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'wisdom', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People and Work.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Identify and describe a range of professions and career paths, explaining the skills and training each one requires
Explain how personal interests and strengths can guide career choices
Read a passage about careers and jobs and answer comprehension questions at different thinking levels
Recognise and form compound nouns in their three forms: closed (e.g. toothbrush), hyphenated (e.g. mother-in-law), and open (e.g. tuition fees)
Change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words
Use reported speech accurately in both writing and conversation
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the present continuous to describe future arrangements (e.g. She is starting her new job on Monday)
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Identify profession suffixes such as -ist, -ian, -or, and -er and use them to build job titles
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: People and Work: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit the focus is on people and work. You will explore the many different jobs and professions that keep communities running, from physicians and lawyers to food vendors and commentators. You will learn how people choose their careers, what skills different jobs require, and how the world of work connects to ideas of fairness, justice, and community responsibility.
Along the way, you will strengthen your grammar by learning about compound nouns, the combinations of two or more words that create a single noun with a specific meaning. You will also practise reported speech, which allows you to tell someone what another person said without using their exact words. Both of these grammar skills are essential for clear, mature writing and speaking.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Identify and describe a range of professions and career paths, explaining the skills and training each one requires
Explain how personal interests and strengths can guide career choices
Read a passage about careers and jobs and answer comprehension questions at different thinking levels
Recognise and form compound nouns in their three forms: closed (e.g. toothbrush), hyphenated (e.g. mother-in-law), and open (e.g. tuition fees)
Change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words
Use reported speech accurately in both writing and conversation
Use the present continuous to describe future arrangements (e.g. She is starting her new job on Monday)
Identify profession suffixes such as -ist, -ian, -or, and -er and use them to build job titles
Plan and write a job advertisement or a career presentation using persuasive and informative language
Take part in speaking and listening activities including a career interview role-play and a job description guessing game
Evaluate your own learning by completing a set of self-assessment statements
Before you begin reading, study the words below. These words will appear throughout the unit. Make sure you understand each one so that you can follow the texts and use the words in your own writing. You will find the complete list with practice activities in your Vocabulary file.
You will also use: script, text, caption, figures, criticism, ammunition, integration, segregation, frustration, tuition, migration, commodity, centuries, allies, presence, vacuole, heredity, item, refuse. See your Vocabulary file for the complete list.
Read the following passage carefully. It describes several different careers and explains how each one helps to build and protect a community. As you read, think about the skills each profession requires and how compound nouns are used to describe parts of the working world.
Every morning in the East African city of Hargeisa, hundreds of people set off to work. Some carry briefcases, some carry toolboxes, and some carry nothing but their knowledge and determination. Each person has a profession, a role that helps keep the community running smoothly. Understanding these roles can help you start thinking about your own future career.

readings.title: People and Work: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
At the local hospital, a physician named Dr Musa begins his shift at seven o’clock. His profession requires years of study. He completed medical school, passed difficult exams, and paid tuition fees that his family saved for over many years. Now he treats patients, reads test results, and gives careful observation reports to his team. Being a physician demands patience, scientific knowledge, and the ability to communicate clearly with people who are worried or in pain. Dr Musa always says that the most important quality in medicine is the wisdom to listen before you speak.
Across the road from the hospital, a young woman named Amal works as a legal advisor at a small law office. Her job involves studying legal documents, understanding the country’s laws, and giving people advice about their rights. She helps families settle disputes, assists business owners with contracts, and sometimes appears in courtrooms to defend her clients. Amal knows that legal penalties can be serious, so she works carefully and pays attention to every detail. She often reminds her younger siblings that a career in law requires strong reading skills, clear thinking, and a deep sense of fairness.
Not far away, in the busy Waheen market, a food vendor named Mama Amal runs a popular stall. She has been selling freshly made sambusas and canjeero for over twenty years. Although she did not attend a university, Mama Amal is one of the most efficient workers in the market. She wakes before dawn to prepare her ingredients, keeps her cooking area spotlessly clean to avoid any contaminated food, and manages her money carefully. Her business skills are the result of decades of experience and wisdom passed down through her family. Mama Amal proves that a valuable profession does not always require a formal qualification but always requires dedication and hard work.
At the local radio station, a commentator named Ishmael records his evening programme. He writes a script for each broadcast, choosing his words carefully so that listeners can understand the day’s events. He reads figures and facts aloud, adds his own commentary, and invites guests to share their views. A commentator must be well-informed, confident, and able to explain complicated ideas in simple language. Ishmael also writes captions for the station’s social media pages, making sure every post is accurate and clear.
These four people have very different jobs, yet they all share important qualities: responsibility, dedication, and a desire to serve their community. Whether you dream of becoming a historian, an inventor, a teacher, or something entirely different, the key is to discover what you love, develop the skills you need, and never stop learning. Your profession is not just what you do for money — it is how you contribute to the world around you.

readings.title: Voices in the Courtroom, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The day of the trial felt different from every other in the town of Luyanda.
Shops opened more slowly. Motorbikes moved more quietly. Even the sky, usually so loud with birds, felt still.
Twelve-year-old Sarah waited outside the school gate, clutching a folder full of papers. Her class was going on a field trip — not to the museum, or the zoo, but to the courtroom.
“We are currently observing a real case,” their teacher had said. “You’ll learn how rights, laws, and profession roles work in society.”
Sarah had never stepped foot in a courthouse before. But she knew this was not a normal case.
It involved her neighbour, Mama Hannah.
Mama Hannah was a local food vendor who often gave Sarah extra cassava or banana when she passed by. She was kind, wise, and popular. But last month, officials came to her stall and accused her of selling contaminated food.
They said her cooking water was unsafe. She was arrested, fined, and told she could no longer sell.
“I refuse to believe she did anything wrong,” Sarah had told her parents.
“They say the test showed bacteria,” her mother had replied.
“She said the officer didn’t have her consent to test her pots,” Sarah insisted.
“She told us,” her father added, “that the food wasn’t even served that day.”
Sarah had listened to every word — and written it down. Now she carried those notes in her folder like evidence.
Inside the courthouse, rows of benches lined the walls. The room was full of murmurs. A judge in a black robe sat at the front. A commentator from the local radio station recorded notes for a short evening script.
The teacher handed out a sheet.
“Today, you will identify: the defense and prosecution; any compound nouns (such as tuition fees and legal penalties); verbs in present continuous; and new profession titles based on suffixes.”
Sarah’s eyes danced across the list.
“The State v. Hannah Mtebe,” the bailiff announced.
A young physician testified first. “The test showed bacteria in the container,” he said. “Food from this water could cause illness.”
“But was food served that day?” asked the defense lawyer.
The physician hesitated. “We’re not currently sure.”
Sarah scribbled: “Observation unclear. Present continuous used.”
Mama Hannah’s commentary came next. She stood tall, her voice calm.
“The officer entered without asking. I never gave my consent. He touched my pots with gloves still dirty from the last stall. I told him: ‘That’s not allowed.’ But he wrote down his criticism anyway.”
Her lawyer nodded. “That’s reported speech. He said, she said.”

readings.title: Voices in the Courtroom, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The teacher leaned over to Sarah. “You’re doing good observation,” she whispered.
The prosecution argued that penalties must be applied equally. “All vendors must be inspected. Legal standards are there for a reason.”
“But,” Mama Hannah’s lawyer said, “the process was flawed. If the action was unfair, the penalty is unconstitutional.”
Sarah whispered to her classmate: “That means it goes against the rules of the country.”
The judge listened quietly, taking notes with a blue pen. His face revealed nothing.
After a break, the students gathered under a tree beside the courthouse.
“I still don’t understand,” one boy said. “If there were figures, like test results, doesn’t that mean she’s guilty?”
“But the figures might be wrong,” Sarah argued. “And even good data needs good methods.”
“It’s like when you study heredity in science,” said a girl. “You can’t just assume something based on one test.”
Their teacher nodded. “Good thinking. Scientific wisdom applies in court too. Look at all sides.”
Back inside, a final witness spoke: a food safety commentator from the city.
“She is efficient, yes,” the woman said. “But during food checks, many women vendors are targeted more. We need more fair integration, not more bias or segregation.”
“Please avoid political speech,” said the prosecution.
But the judge replied, “Injustice is always political. Proceed.”
The trial ended, and the judge said he would return a verdict the next day.
As the students filed out, the teacher asked them to write a reflection.
“Today we saw how laws are made and used. But rules must be fair. When someone like Mama Hannah is punished unfairly, people lose trust. Good observation is like science. It takes patience and care. I want to be a commentator, a legal advisor, or maybe a historian one day — someone who helps stories stay true.”
That night, Sarah told her parents everything.
“She said the officer never washed his gloves. She said she never served that day.”
“She said the criticism was false,” her father added.
Her mother nodded. “She said the penalty wasn’t fair.”
“Exactly!” Sarah smiled. “We all used reported speech. We all noticed the same things.”
The next morning, the town was waiting.
The judge read aloud: “The evidence was incomplete. The officer’s observation lacked protocol. The penalty is dropped. Mama Hannah is free to return to her profession.”
Sarah did not jump or cheer. She just grinned, wide and quiet.
The woman touched her shoulder. “Thank you. Your presence mattered.”
Sarah felt her heart fill. It was a small moment — but powerful.
The next week, the students wrote scripts of the event for a class radio play. Sarah helped add captions under printed photos, and even wrote the commentary for the narrator.

grammar.title: What Are Compound Nouns?
grammar.explanation:
A compound noun is a noun made up of two or more words that come together to create a new word with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns is important because they help us express complex ideas in a simple way. For example, instead of saying “the fees for tuition,” we can say tuition fees. Instead of saying “the room where the court meets,” we can say courtroom. Compound nouns make our language clearer, more precise, and more efficient.
Why Do Compound Nouns Matter?
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A compound noun is a noun made up of two or more words that come together to create a new word with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns is important because they help us express complex ideas in a simple way. For example, instead of saying “the fees for tuition,” we can say tuition fees. Instead of saying “the room where the court meets,” we can say courtroom. Compound nouns make our language clearer, more precise, and more efficient.
Why Do Compound Nouns Matter?
Compound nouns are everywhere in English, especially in the language of work, law, science, and daily life. You will find them in:
News reports: police officer, crime scene, press conference, news headline
Schools: school uniform, classroom, lesson plan, text book, head teacher
Law and government: legal penalties, courtroom, law court, human rights
Health: test results, blood pressure, check-up, health worker
Work and careers: job interview, business owner, fire station, bus driver
The more compound nouns you know, the more precisely you can describe the world around you.
The Three Forms of Compound Nouns
1. Closed Form (One Word)
In the closed form, the two words are joined together as a single word with no space and no hyphen.
toothbrush = tooth + brush
football = foot + ball
classroom = class + room
courtroom = court + room
headmaster = head + master
sunlight = sun + light
newspaper = news + paper
bedroom = bed + room
Closed compound nouns are the easiest to recognise because they look like a single word. You can often find them in a dictionary as one entry.
2. Hyphenated Form
In the hyphenated form, the words are connected with a hyphen (-). Hyphens are used when the compound might be confusing without them or when it follows a specific English spelling rule.
mother-in-law = mother + in + law
well-being = well + being
check-up = check + up
passer-by = passer + by
six-year-old = six + year + old
self-control = self + control
Hyphenated compound nouns are less common than the other two forms, but you should know how to read and write them correctly.
3. Open Form (Two Separate Words)
In the open form, the words are written separately but are understood as a single unit of meaning.
tuition fees = tuition + fees
legal penalties = legal + penalties
bus stop = bus + stop
swimming pool = swimming + pool
ice cream = ice + cream
post office = post + office
fire station = fire + station
test results = test + results
food vendor = food + vendor
script writing = script + writing
migration path = migration + path
police officer = police + officer
Open compound nouns can be tricky because the words look separate, but together they mean something specific. A swimming pool is not just any pool that happens to be swimming — it is a specific type of pool designed for swimming.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are compound nouns? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form Compound Nouns
grammar.explanation:
To form a compound noun, follow these steps:
Identify the two nouns (or noun + adjective/verb). Think about what you want to name. For example, you want to talk about fees related to tuition.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
To form a compound noun, follow these steps:
Identify the two nouns (or noun + adjective/verb). Think about what you want to name. For example, you want to talk about fees related to tuition.
Put the describing word first. The first word usually tells us what kind or what type. In tuition fees, tuition tells us what kind of fees.
Check the form. Look in a dictionary or ask your AI tutor whether the compound is closed, hyphenated, or open. There is no simple rule for this — you learn the correct form through reading and practice.
Use it in a sentence. Make sure the compound noun fits naturally. Example: My parents saved for years to pay the tuition fees.
Common Patterns
Here are some common ways compound nouns are built:
Noun + noun: toothbrush, courtroom, bus stop, football, sunlight
Adjective + noun: greenhouse, blackboard, full moon, high court
Verb + noun: swimming pool, driving licence, washing machine
Noun + verb: sunrise, rainfall, haircut
Verb + preposition: check-up, break-in, workout
Compound Nouns in the World of Work
Here are compound nouns that relate specifically to jobs, careers, and the working world. These are useful for this unit and for your writing tasks.
Reported Speech — Review and Practice
Reported speech is the second grammar focus for this unit. Here is a quick review of the key rules. For a full explanation, see the Grammar Focus section in your Lesson file.
Quick Reference: Tense Shifts
Quick Reference: Time and Place Changes
Exercises
Exercise 1: Identify the Form
Write whether each compound noun is closed, hyphenated, or open:
toothbrush
bus stop
mother-in-law
classroom
swimming pool
check-up
football
police officer
well-being
tuition fees
Exercise 2: Build Compound Nouns
Match a word from each column to make a compound noun. Write a sentence for each one.
Column A: fire, head, script, legal, ice
Column B: cream, station, master, writing, penalties
Exercise 3: Fill in the Blanks with Compound Nouns
Choose the correct compound noun from the box to complete each sentence:
My parents are worried about the ______ for next year.
The doctor said I should come back for a ______ in six months.
The ______ showed that the water was clean.
The ______ sold delicious sambusas at the market.
The judge entered the ______ and everyone stood up.
Every year, the birds follow the same ______ to warmer lands.
Exercise 4: Direct to Reported Speech
Change each sentence from direct speech to reported speech:
The doctor said, “I need to see the test results.”
Mama Hannah said, “I never gave my consent.”
The teacher said, “We are visiting the courthouse today.”
Isaac said, “I will write the script tomorrow.”
The judge said, “The evidence was incomplete.”
Amal said, “I can help you with your legal problem.”
The student asked, “What is a compound noun?”
Sarah asked, “Did the officer have her consent?”
Exercise 5: Write Your Own Compound Nouns
Write five sentences about jobs and the working world. Each sentence must contain at least one compound noun. Underline the compound nouns.
______
______
______
______
______
Exercise 6: Reported Speech in the Story
Go back to the story “Voices in the Courtroom.” Find five examples of reported speech and write them below. For each one, write the original direct speech version as well.
Exercise 7: Spot the Errors
Each sentence has a mistake with either a compound noun or reported speech. Rewrite it correctly:
The fees of tuition are very expensive this year.
She said that she is working at the hospital. (The main verb was in the past.)
He told that he would come tomorrow.
The swimming-pool was closed for cleaning. (Check the form.)
She asked where did I work. (Check the word order.)

grammar.title: Grade 6 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a three-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark six changes and explain how three changes improve clarity and cohesion.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Career Interview Role-Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: In this activity, one person plays the role of a worker and the other plays the role of a journalist. The journalist interviews the worker about their profession. Here is how to play. First, the worker chooses a profession, either one from this unit or any other job they know about. Second, the journalist prepares at least five questions to ask, such as: What does your job involve? What skills do you need? What is the most challenging part of your work? What advice would you give to a young person who wants to do this job? Third, the journalist asks the questions and listens carefully to the answers. Fourth, after the interview, the journalist reports what the worker said using reported speech. For example: She told me that her job involved reading legal documents. He said that the most challenging part was working long hours. Swap roles and play again with a different profession.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise career interview role-play and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Job Description Guessing Game
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
In this game, one person describes a job without saying the name of the profession. The listener must guess which job it is. Here is how to play. The speaker describes three things about the job: what the person does, where they work, and one tool or item they use. For example: This person writes scripts, speaks into a microphone, and works at a radio station. The listener guesses: A commentator! Try to play at least five rounds, using different jobs each time. After each round, the speaker should say one compound noun related to the job.
Activity 3 — Career Day Speech
Imagine your school is holding a Career Day and you have been asked to give a short talk about a profession. Prepare a one-to-two-minute speech. Your speech should include the name of the profession, what it involves, why it matters to the community, and why you think young people should consider it. Practise your speech before you deliver it. Speak clearly, make eye contact, and try to sound confident and enthusiastic. If you are working with a partner, ask them to listen and then report back to you what you said, using reported speech. This is a great way to practise both speaking and grammar at the same time.
Activity 4 — Listen and Report
Ask your AI tutor or a family member to tell you something about their job or their daily routine. Listen carefully without interrupting. When they have finished, write down three things they said using reported speech. For example, if your mother says, “I wake up at five o’clock every morning,” you would write: She said that she woke up at five o’clock every morning. This activity trains both your listening skills and your grammar. After writing your three sentences, share them with the person and check if you reported their words accurately.
Check Your Learning
Now that you have completed this unit, read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it. If you can, put a tick. If you are not sure, go back and review the relevant section before moving on. Being honest with yourself is the best way to make real progress.
I can name and describe at least four different professions and explain what skills each one requires.
I can explain how personal interests and strengths can guide someone’s career choices.
I can identify compound nouns in a text and say whether they are closed, hyphenated, or open form.
I can form my own compound nouns and use them correctly in sentences.
I can change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise job description guessing game and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Job Advertisement and Career Presentation
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Now it is time to put everything you have learned into practice. In this workshop you will choose one of two writing tasks. Both tasks ask you to use the vocabulary, compound nouns, and persuasive language you have been studying. Choose the task that interests you most.
Option A — Write a Job Advertisement
Imagine you are the manager of a business and you need to hire a new worker. Write a job advertisement that describes the role and encourages the right person to apply. Your advertisement should be between 150 and 200 words.
Your advertisement should include:
A clear job title (use a compound noun if possible, such as project manager, food inspector, or radio presenter)
A short description of the company or organisation
A list of responsibilities (what the person will do each day)
A list of requirements (what qualifications, skills, or experience the person needs)
Information about the salary, working hours, or other benefits
A final sentence that encourages people to apply, using persuasive language
Here is a model to guide you:
Option B — Prepare a Career Presentation
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 6 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about job advertisement and career presentation.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence; considered viewpoint where relevant; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a clear structure, topic sentences and supporting evidence, then use the word bank and source models.
writing.extension: Synthesize details from more than one text, vary sentence structures, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Before you share your work, check it against this list:
My writing has a clear title and is well organised into paragraphs.
I have used at least three compound nouns correctly.
I have included at least one example of reported speech.
My language is clear and persuasive — it makes the reader interested in the job.
I have used correct spelling, punctuation, and grammar throughout.
My writing is the right length (150 to 200 words).
I have used vocabulary from this unit where it fits naturally.
Peer Feedback
Exchange your writing with a partner. Read their work carefully and give feedback using these questions:
Is the job or profession clearly described? Could you picture what the person does?
Can you find at least two compound nouns? Write them down.

Check Your Learning

quizzes.question: What does 'wisdom' mean?
quizzes.options: A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something. | The ability to make good choices and decisions based on knowledge and experience. | The act of watching something carefully to learn more about it. | The written words in a book, article, or message.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The ability to make good choices and decisions based on knowledge and experience.
quizzes.explanation: My grandmother has a lot of wisdom because she has lived for many years and learned many things.

quizzes.question: What does 'script' mean?
quizzes.options: A short description or title that explains a picture or a video. | A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something. | A written text for a play, movie, or show that tells what the characters say and do. | The act of watching something carefully to learn more about it.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A written text for a play, movie, or show that tells what the characters say and do.
quizzes.explanation: The actors read their lines from the script during the rehearsal.

quizzes.question: What does 'text' mean?
quizzes.options: A short description or title that explains a picture or a video. | Numbers or shapes that represent something. | The act of watching something carefully to learn more about it. | The written words in a book, article, or message.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The written words in a book, article, or message.
quizzes.explanation: I read the text in my English book to learn new words.

quizzes.question: What does 'commentary' mean?
quizzes.options: A short description or title that explains a picture or a video. | A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something. | Numbers or shapes that represent something. | Permission to do something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher gave a commentary on the story we read in class.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'People and Work: source text 2': Dr Musa always says that the most important quality in medicine is the _____ to listen before you speak.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Dr Musa always says that the most important quality in medicine is the wisdom to listen before you speak.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'People and Work: source text 2': Ishmael also writes _____ for the station’s social media pages, making sure every post is accurate and clear.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Voices in the Courtroom, part 1': “She said the officer didn’t have her _____ to test her pots,” Sarah insisted.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between a job and a profession?
What compound nouns can you find in the reading passage?
Which of the four careers described interests you most, and why?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my answers for Exercises 3 and 4?
Can you give me more practice sentences for changing direct speech to reported speech?
I am confused about when to use said and when to use told. Can you explain again?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my job advertisement and tell me if it is clear and persuasive?
Can you suggest compound nouns I could add to my career presentation?
Can you help me turn my direct quotes into reported speech?
Can you read my writing and check for grammar mistakes?

I am not sure about some of my Check Your Learning answers. Can you quiz me on compound nouns and reported speech?
Can you give me a short passage and ask me to find all the compound nouns?
Can you test me by giving me direct speech sentences to change into reported speech?

What is the difference between a caption and a commentary?
Can you give me more example sentences with these words?
Can you check my sentences and tell me if they are correct?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between segregation and integration with an example?
What does consent mean in different situations?
Can you quiz me on these words?

What is the difference between migration and immigration?
Can you explain vacuole in a way that is easy to understand?
Can you give me a sentence using commodity that is about daily life?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between refuse as a verb and refuse as a noun?

Can you help me use these words in a paragraph about a conflict and its resolution?

Can you quiz me on the vocabulary words?

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The ability to make good choices and decisions based on knowledge and experience. My grandmother has a lot of wisdom because she has lived for many years and learned many things.
A written text for a play, movie, or show that tells what the characters say and do. The actors read their lines from the script during the rehearsal.
The written words in a book, article, or message. I read the text in my English book to learn new words.
A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something. The teacher gave a commentary on the story we read in class.
The act of watching something carefully to learn more about it. During our science class, we made an observation about how plants grow in sunlight.
A short description or title that explains a picture or a video. The caption under the photo tells us what is happening in the picture.
Numbers or shapes that represent something. In maths class, we learned how to add and subtract different figures.
Permission to do something. You need your parents’ consent to go on the school trip.
The action of protecting someone or something from harm or danger. The team worked hard on their defense to stop the other team from scoring.
Related to the law or rules that people must follow. It is legal to ride a bike on the road if you follow the rules.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and describe at least four different professions and explain what skills each one requires.

selfAssessment.statement: I can form my own compound nouns and use them correctly in sentences.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can report questions using asked + if/whether or asked + question word + statement order.
